retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Permanent Dentition -- Physiologic Variants
retrieved_docs.content: It is argued that the use of fire to cook and soften food during human evolution resulted in a reduced dependency on teeth for survival, which has been linked to a decreased reliance on all tooth types, especially the third molars. This, combined with a decrease in mandibular size, is hypothesized to have increased the prevalence of agenesis and impaction of the third molars in human populations [16] .

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Tooth -- Development
retrieved_docs.content: Two main sets of dentition exist: a primary dentition (colloquially referred to as baby teeth) and an adult or permanent dentition. Under normal physiological conditions, a human has 20 deciduous (baby) teeth and 32 permanent teeth (see Image. Permanent Teeth, Viewed From the Right).

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Teeth -- Embryology
retrieved_docs.content: Teeth form via the process of odontogenesis. Human dentition is diphyodont, meaning there are two generations of functional teeth during life: deciduous teeth and permanent teeth. Humans have 20 deciduous, or temporary baby, teeth that get replaced by 32 succedaneous, or permanent adult, teeth. Around the sixth- to eight-week of embryonic development, the odontogenesis of deciduous teeth begins. In the twentieth week of embryonic development, the odontogenesis of permanent teeth begins. Every tooth develops from the ectoderm and the ectomesenchyme. The enamel arises from the ectoderm. The dentin, cementum, periodontal ligament, and pulp arise from the ectomesenchyme, which comprises the transformation of neural crest cells into developing maxillary and mandibular arches. [3] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Teeth -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The teeth are multifunctional appendages that essential in basic human functions, like eating and speech. Teeth are composed of multiple unique tissues with varying density and hardness that allows them to tolerate the significant forces and wear of mastication. They are attached to the maxilla (upper jaw) and the mandible (lower jaw) of the mouth. Humans have four different types of teeth that each have a specific function, which is classified by structure and location. Also, humans have two generations of teeth throughout a lifetime: 20 primary teeth and 32 permanent teeth. [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Permanent Dentition -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Humans have two sets of teeth during their lifetime: the initial deciduous (primary) teeth and the successive permanent (secondary) teeth. [1] There are typically 20 deciduous teeth divided evenly across the maxilla and mandible. The deciduous teeth eventually exfoliate and are replaced by 32 permanent teeth: 16 in the maxilla and 16 in the mandible. Permanent teeth are classified as incisors, canines, premolars, and molars, while primary teeth do not include premolars.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tooth Eruption -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The periodontal ligament is believed to mediate supraosseous events during tooth eruption. [1] In contrast, intraosseous events, which involve changes within the bone itself, are not directly influenced or regulated by the actions of the periodontal ligament. [1] These events primarily involve bone remodeling mechanisms and the creation of the eruption pathway.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tooth Eruption -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Tooth eruption is when a developing tooth moves from its initial nonfunctional position within the alveolar bone to its final functional location within the oral cavity. [1] Notably, the term eruption should not be mistaken for emergence, as the latter refers explicitly to the point at which any portion of the tooth's crown becomes visible through the gingiva. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tooth Eruption -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The phenomenon of bone resorption offers a distinct pathway for the correct emergence and alignment of a developing tooth. The force propelling the tooth toward the occlusal plane along this path is believed to originate from bone apposition occurring apically to the developing tooth. [3]  The onset of eruptive movements typically coincides with the initiation of  root formation. [3] Root growth is not the primary cause of tooth eruption, although it may have a role in the process. This is evident as teeth can still erupt without fully formed roots. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Tooth -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Teeth serve multiple functions beyond mastication, including shaping the kinetics of phonation, breathing, maintaining a patent airway, and serving as a foundation for the vertical dimensions of the face. [1] The maxilla and mandible, which together form the jaw, contain alveolar bone, a thick ridge of bone that forms the sockets of the teeth. Appropriate size and jaw positioning are critical in developing a proper bite (occlusion) and subsequent mastication. As discussed later in detail, certain teeth have specialized roles in chewing, with the entire group functioning as a dynamic entity. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Permanent Dentition -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: Enamel has an organized and complex structure, fundamental to its mechanical performance. The hydroxyapatite crystals align to form enamel rods (previously known as prisms) enclosed in a sheath of the organic matrix. [4] The rods follow an undulating path from the enamel-dentine junction (EDJ) towards the outer occlusal surface, where their arrangement is more parallel. [5] The inner enamel layer's decussating pattern creates Hunter-Schreger bands (viewable under reflected light), accounting for its resistance to crack formation. Within the oral cavity, dental enamel can repeatedly withstand masticatory forces of up to 770 newtons, owing to its resilience. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Human Trafficking -- History and Physical -- Head-to-Toe Assessment
retrieved_docs.content: Growth and development abnormalities in children, dental cavities, or misaligned poorly formed teeth.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Permanent Dentition -- Physiologic Variants
retrieved_docs.content: Dilaceration describes an unusually abrupt angle between tooth crown and root or within the root itself, resulting from trauma during the development of the tooth. Further anomalies of the dental hard tissues include gemination, where a single tooth forms two crowns. This is a consequence of the tooth bud's partial division, resulting in a bifid crown but retaining the normal number of expected teeth within the dental arch. [30] However, fusion leads to one less tooth within the dental arch as it comprises two separate developing tooth germs which join together. [31] Concrescence is a rare phenomenon where adjacent teeth are united by cementum. [32]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tooth Eruption -- Structure and Function -- Bite forces theory
retrieved_docs.content: Recent theories suggest that the soft tissues within the dental follicle can detect bite forces, which are believed to have a crucial role in directing bone remodeling processes and influencing tooth eruption. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tooth Eruption -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: Two crucial factors aid in the process of tooth eruption. First, the permanent teeth require the removal of bone and primary roots. Second, soft tissue alterations are necessary, followed by the application of force that propels the tooth toward the occlusal surface, directing it along an established eruption pathway. [4] Despite significant progress, the exact mechanisms regulating these events are still not fully comprehended. Nonetheless, valuable insights have been gleaned from studies involving various species and clinical experiences.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Risorius Muscle -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: If you are smiling right now, you are likely engaging the risorius muscle – that is, if you even have a risorius muscle. Commonly known as the smiling muscle, it serves as a muscle of facial expression. As alluded to here, it is not present in all people, and it is believed to be unique only to the Hominines, or the African great apes and humans, evolving in our common ancestor. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Tooth -- Development
retrieved_docs.content: The process of tooth development begins with the tissues of the first pharyngeal arch. It is a complex process resulting from interactions between the oral cavity ectoderm, which gives rise to cells that produce enamel. The neural crest ectomesenchyme gives rise to tooth structures other than enamel. Primary dentition begins to form in utero between 6 and 8 weeks of gestation. Permanent teeth begin to form in utero at 20 weeks of gestation. If the structures do not start to develop at or near these times, they do not develop at all. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 72
retrieved_docs.content: The category “diseases of mouth; excluding dental” is classified as a subcategory of “اِضطِراب الغدة اللُّعابِيَّة” (salivary gland disorder), indicating that non‑dental oral diseases are considered part of the broader spectrum of salivary gland pathology. This relationship highlights the importance of evaluating salivary gland function when assessing oral conditions that are not of dental origin, thereby guiding more comprehensive diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 244
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is classified as a dental procedure, indicating that it has a relationship with the broader category of dental procedures. This connection underscores that the act of extracting a single tooth is a specific type of dental intervention within the overall field of dental procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 249
retrieved_docs.content: Dental procedures are performed directly on zuby mléčné (the primary or “milk” teeth), indicating that therapeutic dental interventions often target the primary dentition. Likewise, disorders of teeth and jaw are located in zuby mléčné, showing that many oral pathologies also affect these early‑life teeth. Together, these relationships emphasize that both clinical treatments and disease processes in pediatric dentistry are closely linked to the primary teeth, underscoring the importance of careful assessment and management of zuby mléčné in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 250
retrieved_docs.content: The extraction of a single tooth is a direct dental procedure performed on the zuby (teeth), involving the removal of one tooth from the oral cavity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 225
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is a direct procedure performed on zuby (teeth), indicating that the teeth are the specific anatomical site for this dental intervention. Disorders of teeth and jaw are described as having zuby as their parent category, meaning that conditions affecting the teeth and jaw are organized under the broader classification of zuby. Consequently, zuby functions both as the procedural target for single‑tooth extraction and as the foundational element within the hierarchy of dental and jaw disorders, underscoring its central importance in dental treatment and pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 243
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of teeth and jaw are classified under the broader parent category zuby and are specifically associated with the anatomical location of zuby mléčné, the primary (milk) teeth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2709
retrieved_docs.content: Natural and semisynthetic estrogens, plain, are classified as members of the estradiol group.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 226
retrieved_docs.content: Dental procedures are directly performed on zuby mléčné, indicating that primary (baby) teeth serve as the specific sites for these dental interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 362
retrieved_docs.content: Other bone disease and musculoskeletal deformities are associated with bone resorption, indicating that these conditions often involve increased breakdown of bone tissue, which contributes to skeletal weakening and structural abnormalities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2181
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland is classified as a malignant tumor in the sublingual gland (ورم خبيث في الغدة تحت اللسان), indicating that this metastatic cancer represents a specific form of malignant neoplasm affecting the sublingual salivary gland. Recognizing this relationship clarifies the pathological nature and precise anatomical location of the disease, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and therapeutic planning in patients with salivary gland malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1906
retrieved_docs.content: The head of the first metatarsal bone is a distinct anatomical region of the foot that is defined by laterality, indicating that it can be identified as belonging to either the left or right side.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2179
retrieved_docs.content: Melanomas of skin are malignant neoplasms that arise from melanocytes in the cutaneous tissue and represent a specific form of skin cancer. سرطان الشفة الخبيث, which translates to malignant lip cancer, is a distinct malignant tumor that originates in the lip and is classified as an oral cavity cancer. Although both conditions are cancers, they affect different anatomical sites and are not subtypes of one another; each requires separate diagnostic and therapeutic approaches tailored to its location and biological behavior. Understanding the differences between melanomas of skin and سرطان الشفة الخبيث is important for accurate diagnosis, appropriate treatment planning, and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2732
retrieved_docs.content: Accidental physical contact (event) is described as being due to loss of teeth caused by an accident, indicating that dental injury resulting from an accident can lead to unintended physical contact.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 797
retrieved_docs.content: particular bones) are nested under a universal category of late effects, which also encompasses non‑fracture injuries such as burns and frostbite. Recognizing these relationships aids clinicians in systematically assessing and managing the long‑term sequelae of diverse traumatic injuries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 0 -- Community 19
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is a dental procedure that directly targets teeth (zuby) and is recognized as part of the broader set of dental procedures. Dental procedures themselves are also performed directly on deciduous teeth (zuby mléčné). Disorders of teeth and jaw are categorized under the parent concept of teeth (zuby) and are specifically located in the deciduous dentition (zuby mléčné). Consequently, both the extraction of a single tooth and other dental procedures may be applied to permanent and baby teeth, while disorders of the teeth and jaw primarily affect the baby teeth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2316
retrieved_docs.content: The cervical canal structure is anatomically situated within the structure of the internal os, with the internal os serving as the parent structure that encompasses the cervical canal.
